item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good basic idea, but could use a rewrite.

+ show block
– hide block

The subject is in his mid to late 20's and wears a black wet suit with waterproof fingerless gloves

All the time? Can he not change his clothes?

in a deep carnal tone of voice

What does this mean? Is there are more scientific way to say it?

a strange localized incident

Are containment procedures written for Foundation personnel? Then why specify the incident was "strange". If it weren't strange, it wouldn't be documented at all.

approximately 11 feet off the floor

Why use imperial measurements?

My dream had become a living nightmare.

Bad creepypasta cliche alert.

item.title: Young and Under 30 review: SCP-2836
item.content:encoded:
I'm here from the Young and Under 30 page with a review.

+ show block
– hide block

Your description is sometimes overlong and pretty severely lacking in clinical tone. Deep carnal tone, bouts of angst, shying away, washed out speech: these are much more poetic than technical. The length issue comes from a tendency to add in unnecessary adjectives and prepositional phrases.

As for the rest of it… It's a really basic initial idea. The physics of it is somewhat interesting, but it doesn't really expand it much. I have mixed feelings on the interview. Some aspects of it don't seem believable (their relationship/use of the boat doesn't seem realistic, e.g. Winning such different contests, I'm pretty sure you don't wait to name a boat, was he sleeping in a wetsuit?) I did enjoy the researchers banter and feel for the kid, even if the accident feels underthought.

The notes after the interview also seem underthought. I don't follow the doctor's logic and it isn't very scientific or psychiatric. Saying this is psychosomatic doesn't make sense, since he's affecting way more than his body. They seem to be making a huge leap by thinking the boat part will accomplish anything, and it doesn't seem to?

Overall, -1. You need a major check for clinical tone, and then some story elements need to be tightened up to be more realistic/believable. I think there might be something here, but the kids dramatic background and the researcher's notes and actions feel half-baked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is definitely a very fun and creative concept. It's very refreshing to have an SCP's condition sort of explained rather than the tired old "I was just born this way" or "I woke up one day and my hands were made of spaghetti". His anomalous condition is specifically tied into an important aspect of his life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given that the "Caucasian" aspect is not even a dominating aspect in the article, it is harsh to simply vote based on a small detail that matters not in the greater scheme of things.

Although I'm not the author, I invite you to elaborate on this "Caucasian meme".

Do you mean the tendency to call any white person "Caucasian" even though the term strictly refers to people descended from the Caucasus region (Georgia, Armenia etc.)?

Or do you mean the tendency to feature Caucasians (i.e. white people) in SCP writing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting because the caucasian meme is overrated

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually don't mean for the ellipses to over-dramatize the character, but rather to simulate elapsed moments between the subject's breathing during the interview. Keep in mind he is only able to inhale through his nose rather than his mouth which would likely be more challenging. Plus, I imagine continuously interjecting the phrase "audible breathing" or something similar in brackets would have been far more bothersome, don't you think? Either way, appreciate the honest feedback ;-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interview kills this for me - for starters, this goes seriously overboard on ellipses, it makes it read overly-dramatic to the point of narm, and I can't take anything seriously.

Second, I think the character themselves could use more development - as the interview seems more of a vehicle for delivering exposition than it does a solid conversation. Nothing about this made me care about the character, his situation/dead family/anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it overall. No voting, only because the entire back story is pretty much told and explained through the interview.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy what you've done here! I think that it could use some light grammatical touches in the interview (ie "…I'm just…bored." should probably be "… I'm just… bored.") but other than really nitpicky stuff like that, I think it flows well and tells a cohesive, cool story. The end leaves me wanting for another interview log, so including that may make the SCP feel more complete?

+1
